title: On the Genealogy of Morality
subtitle: A Polemic
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
originalTitle: Zur Genealogie der Moral: Eine Streitschrift
themes: The historical origins of moral concepts as products of power struggles
Ressentiment as the psychological engine of the slave revolt in morals
Bad conscience as cruelty turned inward by the constraints of civilization
The creditor-debtor relationship as the prototype of guilt and punishment
Ascetic ideals as a paradoxical strategy of the weak will to power
The priestly caste as a type that uses suffering to gain power over the suffering
The genealogical method as an alternative to both transcendental and utilitarian ethics
The self-overcoming of morality through the will to truth it itself bred
awards: Widely regarded as Nietzsche's greatest and most systematic work
Listed in the Western Canon by Harold Bloom
Influenced Michel Foucault's genealogical method in Discipline and Punish and The History of Sexuality
Called 'one of the first and still subtlest of the Darwinian investigations of the evolution of ethics' by Daniel Dennett
Foundation text for post-structuralist ethics and critical theory
excerpt: On the Genealogy of Morality is Nietzsche's most systematic and analytically powerful work — a devastating genealogical critique that traces our moral concepts of good and evil, guilt, bad conscience, and ascetic ideals back to their historical origins in cruelty, debt, and the will to power of both the strong and the weak.
